narration.default: Imagine a sneaky burglar tip-toeing past your bedroom door at night. You can't see them in the dark, and a regular button is no help — they're not going to politely press it. So we'll build something cleverer: a sensor that watches silently, and a buzzer that screams the moment anything moves. A real security alarm, made of two components and a few blocks. Make sure Arduino Uno is selected and you're in Upload mode. If you haven't done the Blink an LED and Button-controlled LED tutorials yet, finish those first — we'll build on what they taught.

Open the Arduino category and drag the setup-and-loop block into the workspace. You've done this in every Arduino tutorial so far — setup runs once when the program starts, loop runs forever while the Arduino has power. The alarm will live inside loop, checking and re-checking the sensor as long as the Arduino is on.

Drag a pinMode block into setup. Set pin to 2 and mode to input. Pin 2 is going to listen — it'll wait for the motion sensor to tell it whether anything is moving. Same idea you used with the button on pin 2 last time, just a different kind of sensor plugged in.

Drag a second pinMode block below the first. Set pin to 9 and mode to output. Pin 9 will send signals out to the buzzer — that's how the alarm makes its sound. Listening on one pin, speaking on another. Sense and control, side by side.

A button is obvious — a finger pushes it down, a spring pushes it back. You can see the mechanism. But a motion sensor has no moving parts. Nothing presses anything. It just sits there on the wall and somehow knows when a person walks past. So what's actually happening inside? How does a tiny black dome on a board notice that a person crossed a doorway five metres away?

The sensor is called a PIR — passive infrared sensor. Every warm thing — your body, a cat, a candle — gives off invisible infrared. The PIR sits quietly and watches for that warmth to move across its view. Stand perfectly still and the PIR sees nothing — to it, you're invisible. Take one step and the warm patch shifts. The PIR notices the shift, and pin 2 reads high. Stop moving and pin 2 drops back to low. That's all it does — sense moving heat.

The buzzer needs more than a steady high or low to make a sound. It needs the pin to vibrate — flip from high to low to high to low, fast. That's exactly what the block called tone does. The tone block takes two inputs: a pin number and a frequency. Frequency means how many vibrations per second. Set the pin input to 9 and the frequency input to 1000, and pin 9 flips a thousand times every second. The buzzer hears those pulses as a steady note. There's also a sister block called noTone — written as one word, capital T — which stops the vibration and leaves the buzzer silent.

An alarm needs a decision: motion seen — sound it. No motion — stay quiet. That's an if-else, the same one you've seen in Scratch and in the Button-controlled LED tutorial. Open the Control category and drag an if-else block into the loop section. Whatever question we put in the diamond slot decides which branch runs.

steps.title: Drop equals into the diamond slot

narration.default: Open the Operators category. Drag an equals block. Drop it into the diamond slot of the if-else — that's where the yes-or-no question goes. The equals block compares two things; we'll fill in what to compare in the next two steps.

steps.title: Drop digitalRead into the left side

narration.default: Back in the Arduino category, drag a digitalRead block. Drop it into the left slot of the equals block. Set the pin to 2. digitalRead checks what state pin 2 is in right now and reports back high or low. That's how the program asks the PIR: anything happening?

Still in the Arduino category, drag a high block. Drop it into the right slot of the equals block. Now your question reads: is pin 2 high right now? Or in plain words: is the PIR seeing motion right now? When the answer is yes, the if branch runs. When the answer is no, the else branch runs.

Drag a tone block from the Arduino category into the if branch — the top half of the if-else. Set pin to 9 and frequency to 1000. When the if-question's answer is yes — when the PIR has spotted motion — this line runs and pin 9 starts vibrating a thousand times per second. The buzzer screams.

Drag a noTone block into the else branch — the bottom half. Set pin to 9. When the question's answer is no — no motion — pin 9 stops vibrating and the buzzer falls silent. Together the two branches give you the full alarm logic: tone when something moves, noTone when it doesn't.

Drag a delay block to the very bottom of the loop, after the if-else. Set it to 250 milliseconds. Every loop needs at least a small pause — without one, the Arduino re-checks the sensor millions of times per second for no good reason. 250 milliseconds means the alarm still reacts within a quarter-second while letting the Arduino breathe between checks. Get into this habit: every loop you ever write, end it with a delay of at least 250 milliseconds.

steps.title: Add the PIR sensor on pin 2

narration.default: Open the Simulator tab. Click the plus button at the bottom-right and choose PIR. In the pin dialog, choose pin 2 and click Save. The virtual PIR appears next to the virtual Arduino — wired to the same pin your code is reading from.
Click the plus button again, choose Buzzer, set pin to 9, click Save. The buzzer appears next to the PIR. Two components wired up: one that senses, one that reacts.
Click the Start button at the top of the simulator. Your program loads into the virtual Arduino — give it a few seconds. The simulator goes live and the alarm starts watching.

Find the PIR in the simulator. It has a Motion toggle — flip it on. The buzzer screams immediately, because pin 2 is now high and the if branch is firing tone every loop. Flip the toggle off. Silence — pin 2 is back to low and the else branch is running noTone. You just built a working motion alarm.

Try this: change the tone frequency from 1000 to 2000. The buzzer goes from a steady note to a sharper, more urgent shriek. Or add a second pinMode for an LED on pin 13 and a digitalWrite block in each branch — high in the if, low in the else. Now motion lights up the LED and sounds the buzzer at the same time. A flashing alarm light to go with the alarm sound.

Five minutes ago you didn't know any of this. Now you do: a PIR sensor reads moving heat — body warmth crossing its view makes pin 2 go high. tone makes a pin vibrate at a frequency you choose — that's how programs make sound. noTone shuts the vibration off. And the whole alarm is built on the same shape you already know: sense the world with digitalRead, decide with if-else, react with an output block. Sense, decide, react. Burglar alarms, factory machine guards, automatic doors, hand-dryer faucets — every one of them runs this exact pattern. You can write them now.
